metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Intransigent minorities with asymmetric preferences can quietly control outcomes that the compromising majority simply accepts. This helps explain puzzles like how Christianity spread through the Roman Empire. Taleb warns of interventionistas who create catastrophes while bearing no personal cost themselves. His trading career grounds the book: one catastrophic loss erases a hundred profitable quarters.

props.artifactTitle: Minority rule and skin in game
artifactLines: Intransigent minorities with asymmetric preferences can quietly control outcomes that the compromising majority simply accepts.
This helps explain puzzles like how Christianity spread through the Roman Empire.
Taleb warns of interventionistas who create catastrophes while bearing no personal cost themselves.
His trading career grounds the book: one catastrophic loss erases a hundred profitable quarters.

beats.narration_text: The minority rule surprised me: that intransigent minorities with asymmetric preferences can control outcomes the majority quietly accepts. I want to test it. Help me: (1) work a small numeric example showing how a non-compromising fraction flips a market, (2) name the exact condition on preferences that makes it happen, and (3) explain why Taleb's own money-on-the-line trading career gives this claim its moral authority.

beats.narration_text: Taleb, a former derivatives trader turned epistemologist, writes with the precision of someone who learned probability theory not from textbooks but from having his own money on the line. His career arc provides the book’s moral authority: two decades in high-stress finance, surviving because he understood what his PhD-holding competitors did not—that a single catastrophic loss erases a hundred profitable quarters.
